perturbation.original_text: 1.1 In this Agreement, unless the context otherwise requires, the following shall have the meaning as set forth against them below: "Act" shall mean the Companies Act, 1956, and includes, where the context so requires, any re-enactment or statutory modifications thereof for the time being in force. "Affiliate(s)" shall mean with respect to any Party to this Agreement any entity which controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with such Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 In this Agreement, unless the context otherwise requires, the following shall have the meaning as set forth against them below: "Act" shall mean the Companies Act, 2013, and includes, where the context so requires, any re-enactment or statutory modifications thereof for the time being in force. "Affiliate(s)" shall mean with respect to any Party to this Agreement any entity which controls, is controlled by, or is under common financial interest with such Party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of "Act" from Companies Act, 1956 to Companies Act, 2013, and changing the definition of Affiliate(s) from common control to common financial interest with such Party, we create a contradiction. The agreement refers to the Companies Act, 1956 in several places, which will now be inconsistent with the changed definition. Also changing the definition of Affiliate(s) which will create contradictions. The original definition focuses on control (ownership/board control), while the modified one focuses on 'common financial interest', which is ambiguous. It's unclear what 'common financial interest' entails (e.g., shared investments, loans, etc.) and creates in-text contradiction with the use of term 'Affiliate' throughout the document.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 The M&A shall be in accordance with the law for the time being in force in India. Those provisions of this Agreement which are in conformity with the Act, shall be reflected in the M&A.
perturbation.justification: The change in the definition of 'Act' from the Companies Act, 1956 to the Companies Act, 2013 introduces a contradiction with section 3.1, which refers to the 'Act' without specifying which version. As the original agreement referred to the Companies Act, 1956, all references to 'the Act' in the M&A (as detailed in 3.1) would have to be the 1956 Act. However, the modified definition of 'Act' points to the 2013 Act, creating a direct and irreconcilable inconsistency and contradiction in how the law is applied within the agreement. The change of Affiliate(s) definition also creates contradiction as there would be conflicting interpretations of what entities are considered affiliates throughout the rest of the document, creating ambiguity and unenforceability. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type because the changed definition clashes directly with the established understanding and application of the term 'Act' within the document, as expressed in section 3.1. Also changing the definition of Affiliate(s) creates further in-text contradictions.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 MIL/MINDA at its sole discretion will assist without any legal obligation the JVC in the following: - Business contacts in India and the neighboring countries; - Access to Indian Know-how; - Procurement of Indian equipment; - Financing from Indian sources; - Personnel recruitment; and - Administration and fiscal. - Support for additional business from Indian/Foreign customers; and - Setting up of the business of the JVC.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 MIL/MINDA at its sole discretion will assist without any legal obligation the JVC in the following: - Business contacts in India and the neighboring countries; - Access to secret recipes; - Procurement of Indian equipment; - Financing from external sources; - Personnel recruitment; and - Administration and fiscal. - Support for additional business from Indian/Foreign customers; and - Setting up of the business of the JVC.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Access to Indian Know-how" to "Access to secret recipes" introduces a contradiction because 'Indian Know-how' is a broadly understood term related to technical expertise and local knowledge. On the other hand, 'secret recipes' is a more specific term, usually related to food or chemical industries and it is vague, in the current context. Also changing "Financing from Indian Sources" to "Financing from external sources" which can create a contradiction in how both parties will handle finding loan opportunities and the way to proceed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.2 MINDA/MIL, since it will have access to the Technical Know-How which it would not have had otherwise, expressly agree that: (a) during the term of the Agreement, and thereafter for a period of five (5) years after the termination of the Agreement (such termination being termination by IMPCO due to default by MINDA/MIL), MIL/MINDA shall not, directly or indirectly, either alone or collectively or through any of its associates, affiliates, including subsidiaries or any entity owned or controlled by it enter into another joint venture agreement or marketing/distribution agreement with any company or persons in respect of the marketing and sale of goods similar to the Products in the Territory. by using the Technical Know-How
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Access to Indian Know-how' to 'Access to secret recipes', combined with the change from 'Financing from Indian sources' to 'Financing from external sources', creates a contradiction with section 14.2. Section 14.2 refers to 'Technical Know-How' that MINDA/MIL would not have had otherwise. The original text explicitly mentions 'Indian Know-How', which aligns with the 'Technical Know-How' referred to in section 14.2. The changed text, by referencing 'secret recipes' and 'Financing from external sources' dilutes the original intention of section 8.2 in providing Indian know-how and financial support, thereby diminishing the basis for the restrictions imposed on MINDA/MIL regarding the use of Technical Know-How as described in 14.2. This creates an in-text contradiction since the scope of the technical assistance and financing arrangements are changed, thereby affecting the application of the restrictive covenant found in section 14.2.

perturbation.original_text: 16.4 If the JVC fails to sell [one million dollars (US$ 1,000,000.00)] worth of Products in the Territory within [eighteen (18)] months from the date of execution of this Agreement, both the Parties will mutually discuss whether to continue or terminate the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 16.4 If the JVC fails to license [one million dollars (US$ 1,000,000.00)] worth of Products in the Territory within [eighteen (18)] months from the date of execution of this Agreement, both the Parties will mutually discuss whether to continue or terminate the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'sell' to 'license' creates ambiguity and a contradiction. The agreement focuses on manufacturing and selling products. Changing the verb to 'license' implies a different business model (licensing the technology or the right to manufacture to another entity), which is not the stated purpose of the JVC. This creates in-text contradiction as several other clauses refer to selling of products. Also, it's unclear what constitutes "licensing" in this context. Does it refer to licensing the technology to third parties, or does it relate to some other form of product distribution?
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.1 General Purpose: IMPCO and MIL/MINDA have entered into this Agreement for the purpose of establishing the JVC with the main objects of, inter alia, marketing and selling of the Products and providing other associated and incidental services to customers, and undertake other activities as mentioned in its M&A.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'sell' to 'license' in clause 16.4 directly contradicts the explicitly stated 'main objects of, inter alia, marketing and selling of the Products' in clause 7.1. The agreement is focused on selling, and the introduction of 'license' implies a different business model that is not aligned with the original intent and creates an inconsistency in the obligations and activities described in the contract.
